summary: Joel Spolsky introduces CityDesk, a Windows-based content management system built by Fog Creek Software. The product separates content from design, allowing non-technical users to manage websites using a WYSIWYG editor without knowing HTML. CityDesk supports multi-language publishing, regional editions, scheduled publishing, and multiple output formats from a single content source. The post serves as a product announcement ahead of its October 2001 beta launch, emphasizing ease of use backed by a robust database engine.
key_points: CityDesk separates content from presentation, allowing site-wide design changes from a single template modification
The product targets non-technical users who need to manage frequently-updated websites without HTML knowledge
Built-in multi-language support includes a split-screen translation interface with warnings for untranslated content
Multiple output formats (web, printer-friendly, Palm Pilot, large-font, print) can be generated from a single article entry
A database engine enables simultaneous multi-user editing without conflicts
money_quotes: With CityDesk, people who don't know anything about HTML or web servers can easily add, edit, and remove articles from a web site, using a program that is as easy as a word processor.
The real power in CityDesk comes from the fact that you only design the formatting of your site once.
Because CityDesk keeps the text of the articles separate from their design, you can change the design in one place, and every article on your site changes accordingly.
You can even have a whole newsroom banging away at the same time; even a virtual newsroom, with contributors all over the Internet!
Just write the article and set the date range, and CityDesk will never publish it before or after those dates. This is a good way to keep fresh content on your web site when you're on vacation.
You only have to type in the article once, and it will automatically be published in each and every format without any additional work.
key_insight: A content management system that strictly separates content from presentation empowers non-technical users to maintain complex, multi-format websites without understanding the underlying technology.
